title: How to Prepare for a Dynamic In-Class College Presentation
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have been assigned a class presentation. You open PowerPoint or Google Slides and start typing bullet points. You fill the slides with everything you plan to say. By the time you are done, your slides are walls of text, and you are essentially planning to read them aloud while your classmates pretend to listen. This is the most common presentation mistake, and it guarantees a bored audience and a mediocre grade. Here is how to design slides that actually support your message and deliver them in a way that holds attention.

The 10-20-30 Rule

Guy Kawasaki, a venture capitalist who sat through hundreds of startup pitches, developed a rule for slide presentations that applies perfectly to college classrooms. The rule: no more than ten slides, no more than twenty minutes of speaking, and no font smaller than thirty points.

Ten slides forces you to identify what actually matters. You cannot include everything you know about the topic. You must select the most important points and structure them clearly. Twenty minutes is about how long an audience can pay focused attention before their minds begin to wander. If your presentation slot is longer, the remaining time goes to discussion or questions. Thirty-point font prevents you from cramming text onto slides. At that size, you can fit approximately forty words on a slide. That is a feature, not a bug. Slides are visual aids, not your script.

One Idea Per Slide

Each slide should communicate a single idea. If a slide contains three bullet points, each bullet point is a separate idea, and the audience will read all three while you are still talking about the first one. They are not listening to you. They are reading ahead. Split those three bullet points into three separate slides. The presentation will be longer in slide count but feel faster and clearer to the audience.

A good test: can someone who arrived late understand the point of this slide within five seconds? If not, the slide is too complex. Simplify it. Use an image instead of text when possible. A graph that shows a trend is more effective than bullet points describing the trend. A photograph that evokes the emotion you are discussing is more powerful than an adjective on a screen.

Your Slides Are Not Your Notes

Do not use your slides as your speaking notes. If everything you plan to say is written on the slide, you are redundant. The audience can read faster than you can speak. They will finish reading your slide while you are still on the first sentence, and then they will wait for you to catch up. That waiting feels like boredom. That boredom translates to a restless room and a disengaged professor.

Your slides should contain what the audience needs to see, not what you need to say. A key statistic. A diagram. A quote. A photograph. The explanation comes from your mouth. Prepare separate notes for yourself. Index cards, a printed outline, or presenter view in your slide software. Keep your speaking notes off the screen.

Practice Out Loud, Not in Your Head

Rehearsing silently in your head is not practice. Your brain skips over the awkward transitions and the sentences you have not quite figured out. Out loud, you hear the problems. The explanation that made sense in your head falls apart when spoken. The transition between slides feels abrupt. The timing is off.

Practice your presentation out loud at least three times before you deliver it in class. Time yourself. Record yourself on your phone and listen back. You will catch filler words, unclear explanations, and sections where your energy drops. The first practice run will be rough. That is the point. By the third run, you will be smoother, more confident, and more familiar with your material.

Prepare for Questions

After your presentation, your professor and classmates may ask questions. You cannot predict every question, but you can prepare for the obvious ones. What is the most controversial claim in your presentation? What is the weakest point? What would someone who disagrees with your argument say? Prepare answers to these questions in advance. If you get asked something you do not know, say so. "That is a great question. I did not come across that in my research, but I can look into it" is a perfectly acceptable answer. It shows intellectual honesty, which professors value more than bluffing.

Manage Your Nerves

Almost everyone gets nervous before presenting. Nervousness is not a sign that something is wrong. It is a physiological response to being watched. Your body is preparing for a threat. The threat is not real, but the adrenaline is.

Breathe deeply before you begin. Plant your feet. Make eye contact with one person at a time rather than scanning the room. Speak slightly slower than feels natural. Nerves speed you up. Deliberately slowing down counteracts the adrenaline and makes you sound more confident than you feel. The first minute is the hardest. After that, you will settle into your material. The audience is on your side. They want you to succeed. Nobody is hoping you will fail.

The Bottom Line

A strong presentation is not about cramming every fact you know onto slides and reading them aloud. It is about selecting the most important ideas and communicating them clearly. Use the 10-20-30 rule as your guide. One idea per slide. Keep your notes separate from your visuals. Practice out loud at least three times. Prepare for the obvious questions. The skills you build presenting in college will serve you in job interviews, client meetings, and professional conferences for the rest of your career. This is not just a class assignment. It is practice for the rest of your life.
